card.indications: Short-term hemodynamic support in shock states with low blood pressure or low cardiac output after adequate fluid replacement. Use is guided by the cause of shock and current critical-care practice.
card.mechanism: Stimulates dopaminergic, beta1-adrenergic, and alpha1-adrenergic receptors in a dose-dependent manner, increasing cardiac contractility and heart rate and, at higher doses, causing vasoconstriction.
card.dosing: Administer by continuous IV infusion and titrate to blood pressure, perfusion, urine output, and cardiac response. Typical adult infusion ranges are approximately 2 to 20 mcg/kg/min, though higher doses may be used in selected emergencies.
card.routeAdministration: Use an infusion pump through a large vein or central line when possible. Correct hypovolemia before starting. Monitor continuously. Extravasation can cause tissue necrosis and should be treated promptly with phentolamine according to protocol.
card.contraindications: Pheochromocytoma, uncorrected tachyarrhythmias or ventricular fibrillation, and hypersensitivity to product components such as sulfites in susceptible patients.
card.warnings: Can cause severe tachycardia, dysrhythmias, myocardial ischemia, excessive vasoconstriction, limb ischemia, and tissue necrosis with extravasation. Abrupt discontinuation may cause marked hypotension.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tachycardia, palpitations, headache, nausea, anxiety, chest discomfort, dyspnea, and hypertension or hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Ventricular dysrhythmias, myocardial infarction, severe hypertension, peripheral ischemia, gangrene, and extravasation necrosis.
card.interactions: MAO inhibitors markedly increase effects and require major dose reduction. Other sympathomimetics increase cardiovascular stimulation. Beta blockers may alter response; tricyclic antidepressants can potentiate pressor effects.
card.monitoring: Continuous ECG and blood pressure, heart rate, urine output, peripheral perfusion, mental status, lactate when indicated, IV site, and signs of ischemia or pulmonary edema.
card.patientTeaching: Explain that continuous monitoring is required and to report chest pain, severe headache, burning at the IV site, numbness, or new coldness in an extremity immediately.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions during infusion. Fluid and electrolyte management are directed by the shock state and cardiac or renal status.

card.indications: Reduction of elevated intraocular pressure in open-angle glaucoma or ocular hypertension, alone or with other ophthalmic agents.
card.mechanism: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the ciliary processes, decreasing bicarbonate formation and aqueous-humor secretion and lowering intraocular pressure.
card.dosing: Commonly one drop in the affected eye three times daily when used alone or twice daily when combined with an ophthalmic beta blocker, depending on product labeling and prescriber direction.
card.routeAdministration: Wash hands, avoid touching the dropper tip, instill into the conjunctival sac, close the eye gently, and apply nasolacrimal pressure for 1 to 2 minutes. Separate other eye medications by at least 5 to 10 minutes.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to dorzolamide or product ingredients. Use caution in severe renal impairment and in patients with prior serious sulfonamide reactions.
card.warnings: Although topical, systemic absorption occurs. Serious sulfonamide-type reactions, corneal edema, acid-base disturbance in severe renal impairment, and bacterial contamination of multidose containers are possible.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Burning or stinging, bitter taste, blurred vision, tearing, eye itching, conjunctivitis, and eyelid irritation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hypersensitivity, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, corneal edema or decompensation, and serious ocular infection.
card.interactions: High-dose oral salicylates and systemic carbonic anhydrase inhibitors may increase acid-base or electrolyte effects. Other ophthalmic agents require spacing between doses.
card.monitoring: Intraocular pressure, visual symptoms, corneal status in at-risk patients, ocular irritation, adherence, and signs of systemic sulfonamide reaction.
card.patientTeaching: Use on schedule even without symptoms. Do not touch the tip to the eye. Remove contact lenses when directed. Report severe eye pain, swelling, rash, breathing difficulty, or sudden vision change.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions. Hydration should be maintained unless otherwise restricted.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension and symptoms of benign prostatic hyperplasia. Extended-release products are used for BPH and are not interchangeable milligram for milligram with immediate-release tablets.
card.mechanism: Blocks peripheral alpha1 receptors, relaxing vascular smooth muscle and smooth muscle in the prostate and bladder neck, lowering blood pressure and improving urinary flow.
card.dosing: Immediate-release therapy commonly begins at 1 mg once daily and is titrated slowly. Extended-release BPH therapy commonly begins at 4 mg once daily with breakfast. Restart low after an interruption of several days.
card.routeAdministration: Give the first dose and dose increases cautiously because marked orthostatic hypotension or syncope may occur. Swallow extended-release tablets whole and take with breakfast.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to doxazosin or other quinazolines. Use caution with severe hepatic impairment, baseline hypotension, planned cataract surgery, and concurrent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors.
card.warnings: First-dose syncope, severe orthostatic hypotension, falls, priapism, and intraoperative floppy iris syndrome can occur. Extended-release tablets may cause GI obstruction in patients with severe narrowing.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dizziness, fatigue, headache, edema, weakness, nasal congestion, and orthostatic hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Syncope, severe hypotension, priapism, angina, tachycardia, and severe allergic reaction.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives, nitrates, alcohol, and phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors increase hypotension. CYP3A inhibitors may increase exposure.
card.monitoring: Blood pressure sitting and standing, pulse, dizziness, falls, edema, urinary symptoms, and symptom response. Assess prostate cancer risk separately because symptom improvement does not exclude malignancy.
card.patientTeaching: Take the first dose when able to lie down if directed, rise slowly, and avoid driving after the first dose or dose increase until effects are known. Tell the eye surgeon before cataract surgery. Seek care for an erection lasting more than 4 hours.
card.nutrition: Extended-release tablets should be taken with breakfast. Avoid excessive alcohol because it worsens dizziness and hypotension. Follow sodium guidance for hypertension.

card.indications: Treatment of susceptible respiratory, skin, tick-borne, sexually transmitted, zoonotic, and other bacterial infections; acne and rosacea products; and malaria prevention in selected travelers.
card.mechanism: Binds to the bacterial 30S ribosomal subunit and inhibits protein synthesis. It is generally bacteriostatic.
card.dosing: Adult systemic therapy commonly begins with 100 mg every 12 hours, then 100 mg once or twice daily depending on infection. Severe infections may continue twice daily. Malaria prophylaxis and acne regimens use indication-specific schedules.
card.routeAdministration: Take with a full glass of water and remain upright for at least 30 minutes to reduce esophagitis. May be taken with food if needed, but separate from antacids, iron, magnesium, calcium supplements, and bismuth. IV administration requires proper dilution and infusion.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to tetracyclines. Avoid routine use during pregnancy and in children younger than 8 years unless the indication, such as serious rickettsial disease, outweighs tooth-staining risk.
card.warnings: Can cause severe esophagitis, photosensitivity, C. difficile diarrhea, intracranial hypertension, hepatotoxicity, severe skin reactions, and permanent tooth discoloration during tooth development.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, diarrhea, abdominal discomfort, photosensitivity, headache, and vaginal or oral candidiasis.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe esophageal ulceration, intracranial hypertension with vision loss, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, severe skin reactions, and C. difficile colitis.
card.interactions: Antacids and mineral products reduce absorption. Isotretinoin increases intracranial-hypertension risk. Warfarin effect may increase. Enzyme-inducing anticonvulsants may reduce doxycycline concentrations.
card.monitoring: Infection response, cultures when available, bowel pattern, liver function during prolonged therapy, skin reaction, swallowing difficulty, headache or visual changes, and superinfection.
card.patientTeaching: Finish the course, take with plenty of water, stay upright, use sun protection, and separate minerals as directed. Report severe headache, blurred vision, painful swallowing, severe diarrhea, rash, or jaundice.
card.nutrition: Food may reduce nausea, but avoid taking the dose with mineral supplements or antacids. Dairy has less effect than with older tetracyclines but should not be used as the sole liquid for the dose. Maintain hydration.

card.indications: Prevention of pregnancy. Certain products are also approved for premenstrual dysphoric disorder or moderate acne in patients who also desire contraception.
card.mechanism: Suppresses ovulation, thickens cervical mucus, and alters the endometrium. Drospirenone also has antimineralocorticoid and antiandrogenic activity.
card.dosing: Take one tablet daily in the exact sequence provided, at the same time each day. Regimens vary by product, including 21/7 or 24/4 active/inactive schedules. Missed-dose instructions are product specific.
card.routeAdministration: Start according to the prescribed schedule and use backup contraception when required. Vomiting or severe diarrhea may reduce absorption. Do not interchange products without confirming the hormone strength and regimen.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy, smoking in women older than 35, current or high risk of arterial or venous thrombosis, certain thrombophilias, major surgery with prolonged immobilization, migraine with aura, uncontrolled hypertension, breast cancer, severe liver disease, adrenal insufficiency, and significant renal impairment or hyperkalemia risk.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: cigarette smoking greatly increases serious cardiovascular risk in women older than 35. Risks include venous thromboembolism, stroke, myocardial infarction, hypertension, liver tumors, gallbladder disease, and hyperkalemia.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, breast tenderness, headache, breakthrough bleeding, mood changes, bloating, and mild fluid changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Deep-vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, myocardial infarction, severe hypertension, hyperkalemia, liver injury, and severe allergic reaction.
card.interactions: Enzyme inducers such as rifampin, carbamazepine, phenytoin, and St. John’s wort can reduce contraceptive effectiveness. Potassium-raising drugs may increase hyperkalemia. Some hepatitis C regimens are contraindicated.
card.monitoring: Blood pressure, smoking status, migraine pattern, thromboembolism symptoms, mood, adherence, potassium in high-risk patients, and abnormal uterine bleeding.
card.patientTeaching: Take at the same time daily and follow the package directions for missed pills. It does not protect against sexually transmitted infections. Seek urgent care for severe leg pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, sudden vision change, weakness, or severe headache.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if nauseated. Avoid smoking. Potassium-rich foods usually do not require restriction, but patients with renal disease or potassium-raising drugs may need monitoring.
